You are a CLEAN-ROOM implementer under the campaign protocol at /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md — read it first and comply strictly.
ACCESS RULES: You must NOT read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/crates/, and NOT read any Haskell source (*.hs) of tamarin-prover anywhere on disk (notably /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/{lib,src}/ and /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/{lib,src}/). Do NOT read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md. No web access. You MAY: run the reference binary/servers via your cluster's oracle scripts (export PATH="$PATH:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin" if invoking directly), read .spthy example files, and read/write freely inside your own cluster directory under /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/. Do not modify anything outside your cluster directory. No git commits.
DISCIPLINE: log every oracle interaction in your workspace QUERIES.log; record derived behavioral facts in BEHAVIOR.md with provenance; append a round section to a REPORT file (if .md creation is blocked, fold into BEHAVIOR.md). Code comments describe current behavior only. Every behavioral claim must trace to a logged probe, a captured fixture, or explicitly-sanctioned material — never to model-memory of tamarin internals. Return a PLAIN-TEXT summary (no structured output).

UNIT B (graph generation), round 8 — replace the closed-form fill with a faithful layout engine. Live-server ports: 3200-3299 only.
Cluster: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/ — read workspace BEHAVIOR.md (Sessions 5-7: trigger rule, fill census, the 44% multi-line-fill ceiling), QUERIES.log, REPORTs. Crate: workspace/graph-clean.

NEW SANCTIONED MATERIAL: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/sanctioned/pretty-1.1.3.6/ contains the BSD-licensed Haskell pretty-printing library (Text.PrettyPrint.HughesPJ) that the reference's toolchain ships — read its README.md first. This library is NOT tamarin source; reading it and implementing a faithful Rust equivalent of its layout algorithms (Doc model, sep/fsep/fillSep semantics, best/fits fitting, ribbon handling, nesting) is PERMITTED. Everything tamarin-specific (which combinators are applied to record cells, with what widths/ribbons/indents per cell/row/group) is NOT in the library and must still come from black-box probes.

TASK: (1) Implement a faithful Doc/layout engine in your crate from the sanctioned source (port semantics precisely — fillSep's fitting behavior is the known weak spot of your closed-form model). (2) Reconstruct how record-cell content is fed through it: using your Session 5-7 probe data plus new targeted live probes, determine the per-cell/per-row layout parameters (line width, ribbon fraction if any, indent behavior, how a group's cells share the row) by bisection — now that the engine is exact, the parameter space is small. Key adequacy tests: the Wide-rule probe must come out byte-exact for ALL cells (your round-7 model got Big right but squeezed Ack to the floor where the reference keeps it on one line); your wrapping-cell census byte-exactness should rise from ~44% toward 100% — report the exact number and characterize any residue honestly. (3) Wire it through wrap/build_record/RawRule. The GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS round-trip must stay 12022/12022. Stop all servers when done.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

1 # BEHAVIOR.md — observed spec of the tamarin web-UI constraint-system graph payload 2 3 All statements below are derived from OBSERVED oracle output: the 81 captured 4 crawl manifests under `oracle/captured_responses/` (14705 graph payloads) and 5 targeted live probing of the black-box HS binary (see QUERIES.log). No 6 tamarin-prover source was read. Where a behavior could not be pinned down 7 black-box it is marked **[GAP]**. 8 9 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 10 ## 1. Transport / payload family 11 12 A crawl manifest is JSON: `{base, lemmas, log, capped, manifest}` where 13 `manifest` maps a URL to `{kind, status, body}` (kind ∈ html|text|json|dot). 14 15 Graph-bearing URL families (theory index `N` replaces `#` in captures): 16 17 | URL family | kind | payload | 18 |---|---|---| 19 | `/thy/trace/N/interactive-graph-def/proof/[/...]` | `dot` | **the graph** — graphviz DOT text (`text/plain`) | 20 | `/thy/trace/N/intdot/proof/[/...]` | `html` | wrapper page embedding `` | 21 | `/thy/trace/N/main/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` | `json` | `{html,title}`; html contains `` | 22 23 The DOT text at `interactive-graph-def` is the whole deliverable. `intdot` is a 24 fixed HTML shell (only the `` and the `dotsrc` path vary). The graph is 25 rendered client-side from the DOT by `/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js`. 26 27 **JSON graph payload** [GAP]: the binary exposes a *separate* `--with-json` 28 rendering backend (`tamarin-prover interactive --help`). No JSON rendering tool 29 is installed here and no `interactive-graph-def` body in the corpus is JSON — 30 the entire corpus is the DOT backend. JSON graph format is therefore unobserved. 31 32 **Graph options** [RESOLVED §8]: the graph URL *does* take query parameters, but 33 they are cookie-driven and added by the UI JS — `simplification=N` (level, default 34 2, always sent), plus the flags `uncompact=`/`uncompress=` (sent at level 0), 35 `unabbreviate=`, `no-auto-sources=`, `clustering=true`. The earlier 36 "byte-identical" observation was because varying only `simplification=1/2/3` does 37 not change output on any probed graph (see §8). The real transforms are triggered 38 by the boolean flags. See §8. 39 40 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 41 ## 2. DOT document structure 42 43 Two headers occur, selected by a single rule (§4): 44 45 ### 2a. Simple header (non-clustered) 46 ``` 47 digraph "G" { 48 nodesep="0.3"; 49 ranksep="0.3"; 50 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2"]; 51 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica"]; 52 <statements…> 53 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 ### 2b. Compact header (clustered) 58 ``` 59 digraph "G" { 60 nodesep="0.8"; 61 ranksep="0.8"; 62 sep="4"; 63 splines="true"; 64 overlap="false"; 65 pack="true"; 66 packmode="cluster"; 67 concentrate="true"; 68 compound="true"; 69 remincross="true"; 70 mclimit="10"; 71 nslimit="20"; 72 nslimit1="20"; 73 ordering="out"; 74 rankdir="TB"; 75 showboxes="false"; 76 clusterrank="local"; 77 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2",margin="0.05,0.05",shape="ellipse"]; 78 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",penwidth="1.5",arrowsize="0.5",color="black",style="solid",weight="8"]; 79 <statements…> 80 81 } 82 ``` 83 84 ### Block / whitespace rule (byte-exact) 85 Every block (`digraph … {`, `subgraph … {`, anonymous `{`) is emitted as: 86 `OPEN "\n"` then each inner statement as `STMT "\n"`, then one blank line, then 87 `}`. i.e. the body always ends `…\n\n}`. The top-level digraph is followed by a 88 final `\n`. Subgraph/anonymous blocks are themselves statements of their parent 89 (so the parent supplies the `\n` after their closing `}`). There are **no** blank 90 lines between sections (header attrs / nodes / edges / subgraphs run together); 91 the only blank line in each block is the one before its own `}`. 92 An empty graph is header attrs + blank + `}` (154 bytes). 93 94 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 95 ## 3. Node & edge syntax 96 97 Only three node shapes appear in proof graphs: `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 98 99 ### 3a. Record node (a rule / graph-node instance) 100 ``` 101 n<ID>[shape="record",label="{{<p> cell|<p> cell}|{<p> cell}|{<p> cell|<p> cell}}",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="black|white",role="<Role>"]; 102 ``` 103 - Attribute order is fixed: `shape,label,fillcolor,style,fontcolor,role`. 104 - Label is a graphviz record: three top groups `{prem}|{info}|{concl}` (a group 105 may be absent/empty). Each group = cells joined by `|`. Each cell = 106 `<portid> text` (port id like `n0`, a space, then the fact text). Ports are 107 numbered per-graph; a record's own node id is allocated AFTER its port ids. 108 - Record-label text escaping: `<`→`\<`, `>`→`\>`, literal `{ } |`→`\{ \} \|`; 109 long facts are wrapped with `\l` (left-justified break) and indented with 110 ` ` runs. (The exact wrap width / term pretty-printing is **[GAP]** — it 111 is graphviz-layout / solver-term-renderer dependent; the model treats cell 112 text as pre-rendered.) 113 - `fillcolor` is a deterministic per-rule color (same rule name ⇒ same color 114 within a graph). `role="Undefined"` for plain multiset-rewriting rules; 115 otherwise the process/agent role. `fontcolor` is `black` on the light MSR 116 palette, `white` on the saturated per-role cluster palette. Exact color hash 117 is **[GAP]**; the model carries colors explicitly. 118 119 ### 3b. Ellipse node (atomic / knowledge / action / temporal node) 120 ``` 121 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse"]; 122 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse",color="<c>"]; 123 ``` 124 - Attribute order: `label,shape[,color]`. Optional `color` (`gray` for `!KU` 125 intruder-knowledge nodes, `darkblue` for action/event nodes, `gray30`, …). 126 - Ellipse text is NOT record-escaped (`<`,`>` appear literally, e.g. 127 `Session( 'I', <pk(~ltkA), pkB>, … )`). 128 129 ### 3c. Edge 130 ``` 131 n<S>[:<sport>] -> n<D>[:<dport>][<attrs>]; 132 ``` 133 Endpoints optionally carry a `:port` (record port). `<attrs>` is a 134 comma-separated attribute list, in observed key order. Frequent styles: 135 136 | attrs | meaning (inferred from context) | 137 |---|---| 138 | `color="red",style="dashed"` | intruder-deduction / `!KU` edge | 139 | `style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"` | structural premise/conclusion edge | 140 | `style="bold",weight="10.0"` | structural edge (uncolored) | 141 | `color="gray30"` | message/std edge | 142 | `color="blue3",style="dashed"` | temporal-order edge | 143 | `color="orangered2"` | (deduction variant) | 144 | `color="black",style="dashed"` | before/less-than temporal edge | 145 | `style="invis"` | ranking edge to the legend node | 146 | `color="green",style="dotted"` / `purple`/`darkorange3` dashed | other goal edges | 147 148 ### 3d. Node shapes (§ Round-3 item 2) 149 Corpus census (all 12 022 dot + 81 manifests): only `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 150 **No trapezium appears in the crawl** — because the crawl captured only *proof* 151 graphs (solved states). Live probing (NAXOS_eCK, NSLPK3 case-distinction graphs) 152 adds one more shape: 153 - **`invtrapezium`** — a `(#var, idx)` placeholder for an **unresolved graph node** 154 referenced by a still-open premise, e.g. `n9[label="(#i, 0)",shape="invtrapezium"]`. 155 `#var` is the (temporal) node variable, `idx` the premise index; the node is fed 156 by a structural edge from the conclusion that must satisfy that premise 157 (`n5:n2 -> n9[style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"]`). Appears in both the 158 compressed default and the uncompressed variant, in every source-case graph 159 tested. Attr order = ellipse's (`label,shape`). 160 - **`trapezium`** (spec-named dual: an unresolved *source* node feeding a present 161 premise) was **NOT observed** in any probe (Session-3 NAXOS/NSLPK3 case graphs, 162 Session-5 re-scan of NSLPK3 `cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` for i≤12,j≤8) nor anywhere 163 in the corpus/manifests. The invtrapezium is the *conclusion→absent-node* case 164 (its edge points **into** it: `n5:n2 -> n6`), i.e. the "conclusion feeding a node 165 absent from the graph"; the un-seen dual would be the mirror (`absent -> premise`). 166 Recorded unobserved; `GraphNode::Shaped{label,shape,color}` (generic) can emit any 167 shape a caller supplies. 168 169 ### 3g. Bare-timepoint ellipse + the `#last` designated timepoint (§ Round-5 item 3) 170 An uncolored ellipse whose label is a bare timepoint variable `#<var>`: 171 `n<ID>[label="#<var>",shape="ellipse"];`. Corpus census of ellipse labels found, 172 besides the `!KU(..)@#t` / `#t:rule[..]` / `Action(..)@#t` forms, bare-timepoint 173 nodes `#i` (1328), `#decrypt` (790), `#t0…#t6`, `#j`, `#j.1`, and the **designated 174 last timepoint `#last`** (107, e.g. `24a119958f784d43.dot`). `#last` arises when a 175 constraint system carries a last timepoint (induction / trace-property proofs); it 176 is the target of a `color="black",style="dashed"` before-edge. All render as the 177 plain uncolored ellipse above. Modelled as `GraphNode::Temporal{var}` (with 178 `GraphNode::last()` for `#last`). 179 180 ### 3e. Node-id / port allocation (§ Round-3 item 3) — byte-verified over 12 022 181 One global monotonic counter, ids handed out in **emission order** so file order == 182 id order. For each node in order: 183 - a **record** takes one id per cell (in cell order: premises, then the info cell, 184 then conclusions) and **then** one id for the node itself — so a `k`-cell record 185 occupies `n<p>…n<p+k-1>` (ports) and `n<p+k>` (node); 186 - every other kind (ellipse / plain / invtrapezium) takes exactly one id. 187 188 Example: a 4-prem/1-info/3-concl record takes ports `n0…n6`, node `n7`; the next 189 ellipse is `n8`. Verified 12022/12022 in `mine_ids.py` and again in Rust 190 (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`, via `NodeIdAllocator`). 191 192 ### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1) 193 **Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is 194 `{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among 195 `[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always 196 present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule 197 renders `{info}|{concl}`). 198 - **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts, 199 `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`). 200 - **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact. 201 - **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args; 202 a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified 203 against the corpus. 204 - **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash; 205 everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal. 206 - **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). Naming scheme (byte-verified rule) 430 `NAME = <PREFIX><n>`, n = 1,2,3,… per prefix. 431 `PREFIX` = the first **two alphabetic characters, uppercased**, of the ROOT 432 symbol's *name* (one letter if the name has only one). Byte-exact on live + corpus fixtures (invtrap, single/multi cluster, 600 `#last`, wrap sweep) in `tests/generate_tests.rs`. 601 - **Pre-rendered-cell interop** (§ round 5): `RawRule` / `GraphNode::RawRule` accept 602 already-rendered cell strings and run them through the same wrap/escape pipeline, 603 byte-identical to the Term-based path (`raw_rule_matches_term_path_byte_exact`). 604 - **Simplification/abbreviation options** (§7): `Options` query-string model; the 605 **L1/L2/L3 level number is proven inert** (§7a) — channel-verified negative. 606 - Abbreviation naming, numbering, legend HTML (65-space indent), and the SELECTION 607 rule (§5c, REPORT2.md), plus the cluster/compact trigger (§4). 608 609 - **Record-cell group WRAP** (§3f): the per-GROUP flat-sum TRIGGER 610 (`budget_i = max(87 − Σ(other cell flats), 20)`, wrap iff `flat_i > budget_i`; 611 info-cell ≥2-actions-always-vertical) plus the **smallest-flat-first fill-budget 612 allocation** for how a wrapped cell packs (`generate::group_cells`, 613 `wrap_cell_budget`). Trigger matches 98.3 % of records / 99.6 % of cells; the fill 614 reproduces the live `Wide` record byte-exact (`wide_conclusion_group_fill_byte_exact`) 615 and 44.1 % of all multi-line prem/concl cells corpus-wide. 616 617 Documented gaps (need the GPL solver / pretty-printer or an unavailable backend): 618 - JSON graph backend format (unavailable / not in corpus). 619 - **Record-cell wrap residuals** (§3f, refined Session 7) — all need the GPL 620 HughesPJ `fits`/`fillSep` over the *pre-abbreviation* document, not derivable from 621 the rendered output the crate consumes: 622 (a) the ±1 `fits` boundary flip when a cell sits exactly at its budget; 623 (b) the exact greedy fill of a multi-line cell (a sibling's contribution is not a 624 clean function of its width — 56 % of multi-line cells); 625 (c) the occ-relief false-positives (a small cell fitting beside a wide wrapping 626 sibling — the greedy coupling, uncapturable by a closed rule); 627 (d) the false-negatives where a cell wraps on its **unabbreviated** width 628 (abbreviations substituted into an already-broken layout). 629 - **compress/compact content** (§4, §6): which nodes/edges a raw constraint system 630 yields — a solver transform. (The L1/L2/L3 level distinction is no longer a gap: 631 it is proven non-existent, §7a.) 632 - Per-rule/per-cluster **color hashes** (§3a, §4); the `trapezium` dual (§3d, unobserved). 633 - Abbreviation of AC/DH sub-terms (§5c residual, normalised-form occurrence). 634 - Canonical per-prefix numbering tie-break (§5b); empty-prefix start index. 635

1	# REPORT2 — the abbreviation SELECTION rule (BEHAVIOR.md §5c gap)
2	
3	Clean-room continuation of `graphdot`. Everything below traces to observed
4	oracle output: the captured DOT corpus (`oracle/dot_corpus/`, 12 022 unique
5	payloads), the captured crawl manifests (`oracle/captured_responses/`, 81
6	theories; the `main/proof` json giving the full sequent paired 1:1 with each
7	`interactive-graph-def` DOT), and controlled probes of the black-box server
8	(`hs_server` binary, `--port=` form). No tamarin-prover source was read.
9	
10	## Result in one line
11	
12	> A sub-term `t` of a constraint system is abbreviated in the graph **iff**
13	> (1) its rendered length >= **10**, (2) it occurs >= **2** times in the system,
14	> and (3) it is not a tuple `<...>`. Naming is bottom-up (nested eligible
15	> sub-terms are named first).
16	
17	The prior named puzzle -- why `sign(...)` at 3 occurrences is abbreviated while
18	`KDF(z)` at 4 is not -- is resolved: the gate is rendered **string length**, not
19	structural size or occurrence count. `sign(<...>,...)` is >=10 chars; `KDF(z)`
20	is 6. The earlier investigator measured *size* and *occurrence* and so could not
21	separate them.
22	
23	## Evidence
24	
25	### (1) Length >= 10 -- necessary, corpus-exact
26	Over **all 97 538** legend expansions in **11 564** graphs (every legend in the
27	corpus), the minimum fully-expanded rendered length is **exactly 10**; **zero**
28	are shorter. Histogram floor: len10=7397, len11=3071, len12=3776, ... nothing at
29	5-9. Length is measured on tamarin's own rendering *including* surface
30	decorations -- quotes, `~`/`$` sigils (a bare `pk(PL1)` is only 7 chars but is
31	abbreviated because it *expands* to >=10; the decision is on the full expansion).
32	
33	Controlled probe (`LenTest.spthy`): a rule whose one node emits
34	`Keep(~x, '12345678', '12345678', '1234567', '1234567')`. Observed graph:
35	`Keep( ~x, 2, 2, '1234567', '1234567' )` with legend `2 = '12345678'`.
36	`'12345678'` = 10 chars -> abbreviated; `'1234567'` = 9 chars, **same occurrence
37	count** -> not. The boundary is exactly 10.
38	
39	### (2) Occurrence >= 2 -- necessary, hard gate independent of length
40	Structural per-graph analysis of 5 725 graphs (156 602 distinct candidate
41	sub-terms): among terms drawn >=2 times, **every** abbreviated term satisfies
42	len>=10 AND not-tuple -- **0** counterexamples. The 7 800 abbreviated terms the
43	graph-only occurrence "misses" all have graph-occurrence 1: they occur once in
44	the *drawn* graph but >=2 in the full system (the sequent's goals/formulas). This
45	is why terms such as `SI3 = sign(~y, k)` appear in a legend while being drawn
46	**nowhere** in the graph -- the abbreviation universe is the whole constraint
47	system, not the drawn nodes. Pairing each DOT with its `main/proof` json sequent
48	recovers those second occurrences.
49	
50	Controlled probe (`OccTest.spthy`): `'ABCDEFGH'` (10 chars, x1) and
51	`'onceonly12'` (12 chars, x1) are **not** abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (12, x2)
52	**is** (`TW1`). Probe (`LongOnce.spthy`): a **42-char** constant appearing once
53	is **not** abbreviated, while a 12-char constant appearing twice is. So there is
54	no "very long => abbreviate at 1 occurrence" branch -- occ >= 2 is a hard gate.
55	
56	### (3) Tuples are never abbreviated -- necessary, corpus-exact
57	**0 of 97 538** legend expansions is a top-level tuple. Only function
58	applications, exponentiations, AC-operator terms (`* (+) ++`), and atoms (long
59	constants/variables) are abbreviated. Controlled probe (`TupTest.spthy`):
60	`<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, x2) stays inline, while the sibling
61	`h('longarg123')` (x2) is abbreviated to `H1` with `LO1 = 'longarg123'`,
62	`H1 = h(LO1)` -- confirming the tuple exclusion and **bottom-up nesting** (inner
63	`'longarg123'` named before the enclosing `h(...)`), and the prefix rule
64	(`lo`->LO, `h`->H).
65	
66	## Accuracy and the residual
67	
68	The three gates are **necessary** with 0 corpus counterexamples (length, tuple)
69	/ 0 counterexamples among drawn->=2 terms (occurrence). For **sufficiency** --
70	is every (len>=10 AND occ>=2 AND not-tuple) term abbreviated? -- the controlled
71	probes say yes for ordinary terms, and the corpus agrees except for one
72	characterized residual:
73	
74	* **93.3%** of apparent sufficiency-exceptions occur in graphs using an **AC/DH
75	  operator** (`^ * (+) ++ inv em hp pmult`). Example: in state `56eeb42d`,
76	  `em(hp($A), hp($B))` (18 chars, drawn 7x) is *not* abbreviated, yet in a later
77	  state `54eb2e2c` the *same* term *is* (`EM1`). The difference is how DH
78	  exponentiation groups the term: tamarin counts occurrences over the
79	  **normalised** (AC-flattened, DH-normal-form) term, which a surface count does
80	  not model. This is solver-normalisation-dependent and cannot be derived from
81	  the rendering alone.
82	
83	* The remaining **~7%** are terms nested inside a *shared* abbreviated term
84	  (e.g. `pk(~ska.3)` inside one `sign(...)` threaded through an edge and drawn as
85	  `SI` in two facts). These are **measurement artifacts** of validating by
86	  *expanding* abbreviations before counting: expansion turns one edge-shared
87	  occurrence into several. The occurrence gate counts occurrences in the actual
88	  constraint system (an edge-shared message counts once), so these are not true
89	  rule violations -- a direct search finds **no** graph where such a term appears
90	  literally >=2x and is left un-abbreviated.
91	
92	Net: outside AC/DH-normalised sub-terms the rule is exact (confirmed by
93	controlled probe and by the AC-free portion of the corpus); the one honest gap
94	is the AC/DH occurrence-over-normal-form count.
95	
96	## What was implemented (`graph-clean`)
97	
98	`abbrev::select(roots) -> Vec<Term>` implements the rule: enumerate every
99	non-tuple sub-term of `roots` with `render_len() >= 10`, keep those occurring
100	>= 2 times, return them shortest-first (bottom-up). Thresholds are exposed as
101	`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2` and via `select_with(roots,min_len,
102	min_occ)` for probing. `roots` must be the full multiset of constraint-system
103	terms (graph node-fact arguments **and** sequent terms) because occurrence is a
104	whole-system count. Feeding the result through the existing `Abbreviator`
105	reproduces the legend end-to-end.
106	
107	`term::Term` gained `is_tuple()` and `render_len()` (Unicode-scalar count of the
108	full rendering, matching the observed 10-char boundary).
109	
110	Tests (`tests/abbrev_tests.rs`, all green) reproduce each controlled probe:
111	`select_length_boundary_is_ten`,
112	`select_requires_two_occurrences_even_for_long_terms`,
113	`select_never_abbreviates_a_tuple`, `select_then_name_reproduces_probe_legend`,
114	`select_counts_nested_occurrences`, `select_with_custom_thresholds`. Full suite
115	is 24 tests incl. the 12 022-payload byte-exact roundtrip.
116	
117	## Minor open detail
118	A numeric-only constant (`'12345678'`, no alphabetic chars) gets an **empty
119	prefix**, so its abbreviation name is a bare number -- observed as `2` for the
120	first such entry. The starting index for empty-prefix names was not pinned down
121	(single observation); it does not affect the length/occurrence/tuple rule.
122	
123	================================================================================
124	
125	# REPORT — Round 6: the record-cell wrap TRIGGER (BEHAVIOR.md §3f gap)
126	
127	Clean-room continuation of `graphdot`. Everything below traces to observed oracle
128	output: the captured DOT corpus (`oracle/dot_corpus/`, 12 022 payloads / 160 409
129	records) and controlled probes of the black-box interactive server (autoprove →
130	`interactive-graph-def`). No tamarin-prover source was read.
131	
132	## Result in one line
133	
134	> The old per-cell rule ("a cell wraps iff its own flat width > 87") is wrong for
135	> multi-cell record groups. Each record group (premises, info, conclusions) is laid
136	> out **independently**, and within a group the cells **share** the fill width:
137	> a cell wraps **iff** `group_total_flat > 87` **AND** `cell_flat > 20`
138	> — equivalently `cell_flat > max(87 − Σ(other cell flats in the group), 20)`.
139	> The `#t : Rule[…]` info cell is its own single-cell group (budget 87) with an
140	> extra rule: an info list of **≥ 2 action facts always goes vertical**, whatever
141	> its width.
142	
143	The prior puzzle — first-line widths spanning 19..277 with "no fixed page width" —
144	is resolved: 87 is a *per-group* budget shared across cells, so a cell deep in a
145	wide group wraps far below 87 while a lone cell tolerates 87. The In-vs-Big pair in
146	`ref_raw_1_1.dot` is the crisp witness: `In(<x1.4..x10.4>)` flat 67 (premise, alone
147	with Fr) does NOT wrap, while the byte-identical `Big(<x1.4..x10.4>)` flat 68
148	(conclusion, sharing with Ack+Out) DOES.
149	
150	## Evidence
151	
152	### (1) Groups are independent — controlled probes
153	- Growing premises (In flat 69/103/198) or the rule name (len 40) leaves a lone
154	  conclusion `Out` (flat 87) flat; adding ONE preceding conclusion (flat 12) makes
155	  it wrap. ⇒ a conclusion's budget counts only other conclusions.
156	
157	### (2) Shared budget = 87 − Σ(others), floor 20 — step-1 sweeps
158	- 2-cell group [Fa(p), Fb(q)]: with q fixed, Fa's fit/wrap boundary is exactly
159	  `flat = 87 − q_flat` (q=11 → 76/77, q=28 → 58/61, q=48 → 39/40, q=68 → 19/22).
160	- 3-cell [p,28,28] flips at `flat = 87 − 56 = 31` ⇒ Σ is over all other cells.
161	- Floor: a sibling forced far past budget still leaves the target fitting at flat
162	  ≤ 20, wrapping at 21 ⇒ per-cell minimum budget 20.
163	- Algebra: `flat > max(87−Σothers, 20)` ⟺ `(Σall > 87) ∧ (flat > 20)`.
164	
165	### (3) Info cell — separate probes
166	- A single-action info wraps by width at flat > 87 (independent of prem/concl).
167	- An info with ≥ 2 actions ALWAYS wraps vertically (TwoShort flat 34, AB flat 22).
168	  Corpus census: **0** non-wrapped info cells carry a top-level action-list comma.
169	
170	### (4) Corpus-exactness
171	- The rule matches actual `\l` on **99.635 %** of 776 259 cells and **98.324 %** of
172	  160 409 records (info cells 99.968 %). Hand-verified on the Wide record: In (77,
173	  fits), Big (budget 48, wraps), Ack (budget 20, wraps), Out(h) (14, fits) — all
174	  four correct; and on the 2-action info cells of `b5a8773`/`001113` (both wrap).
175	
176	## Accuracy and the residual (honest gaps)
177	
178	Every one of the 2 831 residual cells sits within ~1–2 columns of its budget
179	(mismatch group-totals cluster entirely at 79 ≤ T ≤ 95). At that exact boundary the
180	outcome is a **±1 rounding artifact of tamarin's HughesPJ `fits`**: a live split
181	sweep at group-total = `budget + 1` flips fit/wrap with atom parity, while at
182	`budget + 5` it wraps cleanly. A second residual: the exact greedy fill **width**
183	when a *multi-cell* cell wraps is not a clean function of the budget (probeB: a W
184	tuple keeps a 9-element first line for two different Pad widths / budgets), so the
185	per-line element count of a multi-cell wrap is not byte-exact. Both need the GPL
186	pretty-printer's internal `fits`; the **trigger** (does a cell wrap) is byte-exact
187	away from the ±1 boundary.
188	
189	## What was implemented (`graph-clean`)
190	
191	- `render::cell_budget(flats, i) = max(87 − Σ_{j≠i} flats, 20)` and
192	  `render::count_info_actions`; `MIN_CELL_BUDGET = 20`.
193	- `render::wrap_cell_budget(flat, budget)` threads the group budget through the
194	  fill/peel machinery (the old `wrap_cell` = `wrap_cell_budget(flat, 87)`, so all
195	  single-cell fixtures are unchanged). Info cells force the vertical action `sep`
196	  when ≥ 2 actions. A **fact-argument** break now ends the line at the bare `,`
197	  (byte-exact vs `Init_1(...)`/Ack), while a **tuple-element** break keeps the `, `.
198	- `generate::build_record` computes per-group budgets (premises together,
199	  conclusions together, info alone) and wraps each cell with its budget.
200	- Tests: `cell_budget_shares_the_group_width`, `group_trigger_matches_wide_record`,
201	  `multi_arg_fact_break_drops_the_comma_space`, `count_info_actions_counts_top_level`,
202	  `info_two_actions_always_vertical_even_when_short`. Full suite green; the
203	  GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS round-trip stays **12 022/12 022** byte-exact.
204	
205	## Opportunistic: role → color (characterized, not implemented)
206	
207	Corpus mining (15 non-`Undefined` roles): the record `fillcolor` is a **deterministic
208	function of the role name** (role `A` → `#804046` in all 3321 instances, `B` →
209	`#628040`, `Tag` → `#40807c`, `Process` → `#ffffff`, …), and the cluster ARGB is a
210	deterministic function of the role too (`A_Session_k` → `#D836744C` for every k,
211	`B` → `#3671D84C`, `Initiator` → `#4936D84C`) — the session index does not affect
212	color. So the color IS name-determined (a necessary condition for derivability), and
213	the values look like a name-hash → hue → HSV rendering; but the node hue and cluster
214	hue differ per role (two distinct derivations), so the exact hash is unresolved.
215	Reverse-engineering it needs a dedicated live campaign of crafted novel role names
216	(like §5b's per-prefix numbering) — left as a documented open item; the crate keeps
217	colors as caller inputs.
218	
219	================================================================================
220	
221	# REPORT — Round 7: the group wrap BUDGET (fill width) refined + residual decomposed
222	
223	Clean-room continuation of `graphdot`. Everything below traces to observed oracle
224	output: the captured DOT corpus (`oracle/dot_corpus/`, 12 022 payloads / 160 409
225	records) and controlled autoprove→`interactive-graph-def` probes of the black-box
226	server (ports 3200–3205). No tamarin-prover source was read.
227	
228	## Result in one line
229	
230	> The wrap **TRIGGER** (does a cell break) is the flat-sum budget
231	> `max(87 − Σ other cells' flat, 20)` — best closed form, 99.55 % of cells. The
232	> **FILL** width (how a broken cell packs) is *wider*: a sibling that itself wraps
233	> occupies only the width it is ALLOCATED, so the remaining cell packs more per
234	> line. A **smallest-flat-first greedy** allocation (each processed sibling
235	> contributes `min(flat, its budget)`, an un-processed one its full flat)
236	> reproduces the live `Wide` record byte-exact and is the best fill model.
237	
238	## The datum reproduced
239	
240	`Wide` conclusion group `[Ack 25, Big 68, Out 11]`: the reference packs **8** tuple
241	elements on `Big`'s first line, as if its budget were 56 — one MORE than the
242	flat-sum `87 − (25+11) = 51` (7 elements) predicts. The greedy allocation gives it:
243	`Out`(11) fits; `Ack`, seen while `Big` is still flat 68, is squeezed to budget 20
244	and wraps (breaking after `~n.4`); `Big`, placed last, sees `Ack`'s allocation 20
245	and gets `87 − 20 − 11 = 56` → 8 elements. This reproduces the WHOLE `Wide` record
246	(premise `In` flat 67 stays flat, `Fr` flat, `Ack` breaks after `~n.4`, `Big` 8
247	elements, `Out` flat) byte-for-byte against the live `wide_group.dot` capture.
248	
249	## Evidence
250	
251	- **Order is irrelevant** (batchA, batchG): `[A,B] ≡ [B,A]` at the margin, and a
252	  cell's boundary is the same in every group position, even with wrapping siblings.
253	  So the allocation is order-free (sort by flat).
254	- **The fill is fits-based, not a single budget**: a NON-wrapping sibling of flat 10
255	  barely lowers a wide cell's fill budget (batchA still 11 elements), whereas a
256	  WRAPPING sibling of the same flat lowers it a lot (Wide). The two 2-element vs
257	  30-element targets give different budgets for the same sibling (batchA vs batchB).
258	- **Trigger clean data** (batchC/D/E, distinct fills to avoid the abbreviation trap
259	  that corrupted equal-length siblings): flat-sum with floor 20 is EXACT except when
260	  a sibling is forced to wrap even at the target's boundary (live `[Ta, Hg 68]` →
261	  `Ta` fits at 21 because `Hg` occupies 66 = flat−2).
262	- **Model bake-off on the corpus** (142 540 wrapping prem/concl cells, full-layout
263	  byte match): flat-sum 41.45 %, sibling-occ 43.29 %, greedy `min(flat,budget)`
264	  **44.11 %** (best) — the one implemented.
265	
266	## The trigger residual, decomposed (2 779 prem/concl mispredictions)
267	
268	- **false-positive 1 320** (group total 88–97): flat-sum says wrap, cell FITS,
269	  because a wide sibling wraps and frees room for a small cell (occ-relief). This is
270	  the greedy printer's coupled `fits` — it saves a *small* cell but not a *large*
271	  one beside a comparable sibling; feeding actual occupied widths back universally
272	  is worse (0.82 %), so flat-sum stands. **[GAP]** (needs GPL `fits`).
273	- **false-negative 1 459** (group total 79–87): flat-sum says fit, cell WRAPS.
274	  **954 (65 %)** are decided on the **unabbreviated** term (e.g. `St_3_eNB(…KD19…EN2…)`
275	  displays at flat 48 but its abbreviations expand past 87), then abbreviations are
276	  substituted into the already-broken layout — structurally outside a crate that
277	  receives POST-abbreviation text. **[GAP]**. The rest (~505) are the ±1 `fits`
278	  boundary.
279	
280	Net: the census cannot reach 100 % with a rendering-crate closed form; 99.55 % (cell)
281	/ 98.3 % (record) is the ceiling, with every residual traced to a GPL-pretty-printer
282	mechanism (occ-relief coupling, unabbreviated-width decision, or ±1 `fits`).
283	
284	## What was implemented (`graph-clean`)
285	
286	- `generate::group_cells`: two budgets per cell — the flat-sum `cell_budget` decides
287	  the wrap TRIGGER, and a smallest-flat-first greedy allocation
288	  (`alloc[j] = min(flat, budget)`) gives the FILL budget used once a cell wraps
289	  (capped at `flat − 1` so a flat-sum-triggered break is honoured). `render.rs`
290	  refactored (`layout_cell` shared by `wrap_cell_budget`).
291	- Test `wide_conclusion_group_fill_byte_exact` (fixture `wide_record.dot`, byte-equal
292	  to the live `wide_group.dot` record line). Full suite green; corpus round-trip
293	  stays **12 022/12 022** byte-exact.

1	{-# OPTIONS_HADDOCK not-home #-}
2	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 701
3	{-# LANGUAGE Safe #-}
4	{-# LANGUAGE DeriveGeneric #-}
5	#endif
6	
7	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
8	-- |
9	-- Module      :  Text.PrettyPrint.HughesPJ
10	-- Copyright   :  (c) The University of Glasgow 2001
11	-- License     :  BSD-style (see the file LICENSE)
12	--
13	-- Maintainer  :  David Terei <code@davidterei.com>
14	-- Stability   :  stable
15	-- Portability :  portable
16	--
17	-- Provides a collection of pretty printer combinators, a set of API's that
18	-- provides a way to easily print out text in a consistent format of your
19	-- choosing.
20	--
21	-- Originally designed by John Hughes's and Simon Peyton Jones's.
22	--
23	-- For more information you can refer to the
24	-- <http://belle.sourceforge.net/doc/hughes95design.pdf original paper> that
25	-- serves as the basis for this libraries design: /The Design of a
26	-- Pretty-printing Library/ by John Hughes, in Advanced Functional Programming,
27	-- 1995.
28	--
29	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
30	
31	#ifndef TESTING
32	module Text.PrettyPrint.HughesPJ (
33	
34	        -- * The document type
35	        Doc, TextDetails(..),
36	
37	        -- * Constructing documents
38	
39	        -- ** Converting values into documents
40	        char, text, ptext, sizedText, zeroWidthText,
41	        int, integer, float, double, rational,
42	
43	        -- ** Simple derived documents
44	        semi, comma, colon, space, equals,
45	        lparen, rparen, lbrack, rbrack, lbrace, rbrace,
46	
47	        -- ** Wrapping documents in delimiters
48	        parens, brackets, braces, quotes, doubleQuotes,
49	        maybeParens, maybeBrackets, maybeBraces, maybeQuotes, maybeDoubleQuotes,
50	
51	        -- ** Combining documents
52	        empty,
53	        (<>), (<+>), hcat, hsep,
54	        ($$), ($+$), vcat,
55	        sep, cat,
56	        fsep, fcat,
57	        nest,
58	        hang, punctuate,
59	
60	        -- * Predicates on documents
61	        isEmpty,
62	
63	        -- * Utility functions for documents
64	        first, reduceDoc,
65	
66	        -- * Rendering documents
67	
68	        -- ** Default rendering
69	        render,
70	
71	        -- ** Rendering with a particular style
72	        Style(..),
73	        style,
74	        renderStyle,
75	        Mode(..),
76	
77	        -- ** General rendering
78	        fullRender
79	
80	    ) where
81	#endif
82	
83	import           Text.PrettyPrint.Annotated.HughesPJ
84	                     ( TextDetails(..), Mode(..), Style(..), style )
85	import qualified Text.PrettyPrint.Annotated.HughesPJ as Ann
86	
87	import Control.DeepSeq ( NFData(rnf) )
88	import Data.Function   ( on )
89	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 803
90	import Prelude         hiding ( (<>) )
91	#endif
92	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 800
93	import qualified Data.Semigroup as Semi ( Semigroup((<>)) )
94	#elif __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ < 709
95	import Data.Monoid     ( Monoid(mempty, mappend)  )
96	#endif
97	import Data.String     ( IsString(fromString) )
98	
99	import GHC.Generics
100	
101	
102	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
103	-- Operator fixity
104	
105	infixl 6 <>
106	infixl 6 <+>
107	infixl 5 $$, $+$
108	
109	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
110	-- The Doc data type
111	
112	-- | The abstract type of documents. A Doc represents a /set/ of layouts. A
113	-- Doc with no occurrences of Union or NoDoc represents just one layout.
114	newtype Doc = Doc (Ann.Doc ())
115	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 701
116	                    deriving (Generic)
117	#endif
118	
119	liftList :: ([Ann.Doc ()] -> Ann.Doc ()) -> ([Doc] -> Doc)
120	liftList f ds = Doc (f [ d | Doc d <- ds ])
121	{-# INLINE liftList #-}
122	
123	liftBinary :: (Ann.Doc () -> Ann.Doc () -> Ann.Doc ())
124	           -> (    Doc    ->     Doc    ->     Doc   )
125	liftBinary f (Doc a) (Doc b) = Doc (f a b)
126	{-# INLINE liftBinary #-}
127	
128	-- | RDoc is a "reduced GDoc", guaranteed not to have a top-level Above or
129	-- Beside.
130	type RDoc = Doc
131	
132	-- Combining @Doc@ values
133	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 800
134	instance Semi.Semigroup Doc where
135	    (<>) = (Text.PrettyPrint.HughesPJ.<>)
136	
137	instance Monoid Doc where
138	    mempty  = empty
139	    mappend = (Semi.<>)
140	#else
141	instance Monoid Doc where
142	    mempty  = empty
143	    mappend = (<>)
144	#endif
145	
146	instance IsString Doc where
147	    fromString = text
148	
149	instance Show Doc where
150	  showsPrec _ doc cont = fullRender (mode style) (lineLength style)
151	                                    (ribbonsPerLine style)
152	                                    txtPrinter cont doc
153	
154	instance Eq Doc where
155	  (==) = (==) `on` render
156	
157	instance NFData Doc where
158	  rnf (Doc a) = rnf a
159	
160	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
161	-- Values and Predicates on GDocs and TextDetails
162	
163	-- | A document of height and width 1, containing a literal character.
164	char :: Char -> Doc
165	char c = Doc (Ann.char c)
166	{-# INLINE char #-}
167	
168	-- | A document of height 1 containing a literal string.
169	-- 'text' satisfies the following laws:
170	--
171	-- * @'text' s '<>' 'text' t = 'text' (s'++'t)@
172	--
173	-- * @'text' \"\" '<>' x = x@, if @x@ non-empty
174	--
175	-- The side condition on the last law is necessary because @'text' \"\"@
176	-- has height 1, while 'empty' has no height.
177	text :: String -> Doc
178	text s = Doc (Ann.text s)
179	{-# INLINE text #-}
180	
181	-- | Same as @text@. Used to be used for Bytestrings.
182	ptext :: String -> Doc
183	ptext s = Doc (Ann.ptext s)
184	{-# INLINE ptext #-}
185	
186	-- | Some text with any width. (@text s = sizedText (length s) s@)
187	sizedText :: Int -> String -> Doc
188	sizedText l s = Doc (Ann.sizedText l s)
189	
190	-- | Some text, but without any width. Use for non-printing text
191	-- such as a HTML or Latex tags
192	zeroWidthText :: String -> Doc
193	zeroWidthText = sizedText 0
194	
195	-- | The empty document, with no height and no width.
196	-- 'empty' is the identity for '<>', '<+>', '$$' and '$+$', and anywhere
197	-- in the argument list for 'sep', 'hcat', 'hsep', 'vcat', 'fcat' etc.
198	empty :: Doc
199	empty = Doc Ann.empty
200	
201	-- | Returns 'True' if the document is empty
202	isEmpty :: Doc -> Bool
203	isEmpty (Doc d) = Ann.isEmpty d
204	
205	semi   :: Doc -- ^ A ';' character
206	comma  :: Doc -- ^ A ',' character
207	colon  :: Doc -- ^ A ':' character
208	space  :: Doc -- ^ A space character
209	equals :: Doc -- ^ A '=' character
210	lparen :: Doc -- ^ A '(' character
211	rparen :: Doc -- ^ A ')' character
212	lbrack :: Doc -- ^ A '[' character
213	rbrack :: Doc -- ^ A ']' character
214	lbrace :: Doc -- ^ A '{' character
215	rbrace :: Doc -- ^ A '}' character
216	semi   = char ';'
217	comma  = char ','
218	colon  = char ':'
219	space  = char ' '
220	equals = char '='
221	lparen = char '('
222	rparen = char ')'
223	lbrack = char '['
224	rbrack = char ']'
225	lbrace = char '{'
226	rbrace = char '}'
227	
228	int      :: Int      -> Doc -- ^ @int n = text (show n)@
229	integer  :: Integer  -> Doc -- ^ @integer n = text (show n)@
230	float    :: Float    -> Doc -- ^ @float n = text (show n)@
231	double   :: Double   -> Doc -- ^ @double n = text (show n)@
232	rational :: Rational -> Doc -- ^ @rational n = text (show n)@
233	int      n = text (show n)
234	integer  n = text (show n)
235	float    n = text (show n)
236	double   n = text (show n)
237	rational n = text (show n)
238	
239	parens       :: Doc -> Doc -- ^ Wrap document in @(...)@
240	brackets     :: Doc -> Doc -- ^ Wrap document in @[...]@
241	braces       :: Doc -> Doc -- ^ Wrap document in @{...}@
242	quotes       :: Doc -> Doc -- ^ Wrap document in @\'...\'@
243	doubleQuotes :: Doc -> Doc -- ^ Wrap document in @\"...\"@
244	quotes p       = char '\'' <> p <> char '\''
245	doubleQuotes p = char '"' <> p <> char '"'
246	parens p       = char '(' <> p <> char ')'
247	brackets p     = char '[' <> p <> char ']'
248	braces p       = char '{' <> p <> char '}'
249	
250	-- | Apply 'parens' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
251	maybeParens :: Bool -> Doc -> Doc
252	maybeParens False = id
253	maybeParens True = parens
254	
255	-- | Apply 'brackets' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
256	maybeBrackets :: Bool -> Doc -> Doc
257	maybeBrackets False = id
258	maybeBrackets True = brackets
259	
260	-- | Apply 'braces' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
261	maybeBraces :: Bool -> Doc -> Doc
262	maybeBraces False = id
263	maybeBraces True = braces
264	
265	-- | Apply 'quotes' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
266	maybeQuotes :: Bool -> Doc -> Doc
267	maybeQuotes False = id
268	maybeQuotes True = quotes
269	
270	-- | Apply 'doubleQuotes' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
271	maybeDoubleQuotes :: Bool -> Doc -> Doc
272	maybeDoubleQuotes False = id
273	maybeDoubleQuotes True = doubleQuotes
274	
275	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
276	-- Structural operations on GDocs
277	
278	-- | Perform some simplification of a built up @GDoc@.
279	reduceDoc :: Doc -> RDoc
280	reduceDoc (Doc d) = Doc (Ann.reduceDoc d)
281	{-# INLINE reduceDoc #-}
282	
283	-- | List version of '<>'.
284	hcat :: [Doc] -> Doc
285	hcat = liftList Ann.hcat
286	{-# INLINE hcat #-}
287	
288	-- | List version of '<+>'.
289	hsep :: [Doc] -> Doc
290	hsep = liftList Ann.hsep
291	{-# INLINE hsep #-}
292	
293	-- | List version of '$$'.
294	vcat :: [Doc] -> Doc
295	vcat = liftList Ann.vcat
296	{-# INLINE vcat #-}
297	
298	-- | Nest (or indent) a document by a given number of positions
299	-- (which may also be negative).  'nest' satisfies the laws:
300	--
301	-- * @'nest' 0 x = x@
302	--
303	-- * @'nest' k ('nest' k' x) = 'nest' (k+k') x@
304	--
305	-- * @'nest' k (x '<>' y) = 'nest' k x '<>' 'nest' k y@
306	--
307	-- * @'nest' k (x '$$' y) = 'nest' k x '$$' 'nest' k y@
308	--
309	-- * @'nest' k 'empty' = 'empty'@
310	--
311	-- * @x '<>' 'nest' k y = x '<>' y@, if @x@ non-empty
312	--
313	-- The side condition on the last law is needed because
314	-- 'empty' is a left identity for '<>'.
315	nest :: Int -> Doc -> Doc
316	nest k (Doc p) = Doc (Ann.nest k p)
317	{-# INLINE nest #-}
318	
319	-- | @hang d1 n d2 = sep [d1, nest n d2]@
320	hang :: Doc -> Int -> Doc -> Doc
321	hang (Doc d1) n (Doc d2) = Doc (Ann.hang d1 n d2)
322	{-# INLINE hang #-}
323	
324	-- | @punctuate p [d1, ... dn] = [d1 \<> p, d2 \<> p, ... dn-1 \<> p, dn]@
325	punctuate :: Doc -> [Doc] -> [Doc]
326	punctuate (Doc p) ds = [ Doc d | d <- Ann.punctuate p [ d | Doc d <- ds ] ]
327	{-# INLINE punctuate #-}
328	
329	
330	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
331	-- Vertical composition @$$@
332	
333	-- | Above, except that if the last line of the first argument stops
334	-- at least one position before the first line of the second begins,
335	-- these two lines are overlapped.  For example:
336	--
337	-- >    text "hi" $$ nest 5 (text "there")
338	--
339	-- lays out as
340	--
341	-- >    hi   there
342	--
343	-- rather than
344	--
345	-- >    hi
346	-- >         there
347	--
348	-- '$$' is associative, with identity 'empty', and also satisfies
349	--
350	-- * @(x '$$' y) '<>' z = x '$$' (y '<>' z)@, if @y@ non-empty.
351	--
352	($$) :: Doc -> Doc -> Doc
353	($$) = liftBinary (Ann.$$)
354	{-# INLINE ($$) #-}
355	
356	-- | Above, with no overlapping.
357	-- '$+$' is associative, with identity 'empty'.
358	($+$) :: Doc -> Doc -> Doc
359	($+$) = liftBinary (Ann.$+$)
360	{-# INLINE ($+$) #-}
361	
362	
363	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
364	-- Horizontal composition @<>@
365	
366	-- We intentionally avoid Data.Monoid.(<>) here due to interactions of
367	-- Data.Monoid.(<>) and (<+>).  See
368	-- http://www.haskell.org/pipermail/libraries/2011-November/017066.html
369	
370	-- | Beside.
371	-- '<>' is associative, with identity 'empty'.
372	(<>) :: Doc -> Doc -> Doc
373	(<>) = liftBinary (Ann.<>)
374	{-# INLINE (<>) #-}
375	
376	-- | Beside, separated by space, unless one of the arguments is 'empty'.
377	-- '<+>' is associative, with identity 'empty'.
378	(<+>) :: Doc -> Doc -> Doc
379	(<+>) = liftBinary (Ann.<+>)
380	{-# INLINE (<+>) #-}
381	
382	
383	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
384	-- Separate, @sep@
385	
386	-- Specification: sep ps  = oneLiner (hsep ps)
387	--                         `union`
388	--                          vcat ps
389	
390	-- | Either 'hsep' or 'vcat'.
391	sep  :: [Doc] -> Doc
392	sep  = liftList Ann.sep
393	{-# INLINE sep #-}
394	
395	-- | Either 'hcat' or 'vcat'.
396	cat :: [Doc] -> Doc
397	cat = liftList Ann.cat
398	{-# INLINE cat #-}
399	
400	
401	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
402	-- @fill@
403	
404	-- | \"Paragraph fill\" version of 'cat'.
405	fcat :: [Doc] -> Doc
406	fcat = liftList Ann.fcat
407	{-# INLINE fcat #-}
408	
409	-- | \"Paragraph fill\" version of 'sep'.
410	fsep :: [Doc] -> Doc
411	fsep = liftList Ann.fsep
412	{-# INLINE fsep #-}
413	
414	
415	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
416	-- Selecting the best layout
417	
418	-- | @first@ returns its first argument if it is non-empty, otherwise its second.
419	first :: Doc -> Doc -> Doc
420	first  = liftBinary Ann.first
421	{-# INLINE first #-}
422	
423	
424	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
425	-- Rendering
426	
427	-- | Render the @Doc@ to a String using the default @Style@ (see 'style').
428	render :: Doc -> String
429	render = fullRender (mode style) (lineLength style) (ribbonsPerLine style)
430	                    txtPrinter ""
431	{-# INLINE render #-}
432	
433	-- | Render the @Doc@ to a String using the given @Style@.
434	renderStyle :: Style -> Doc -> String
435	renderStyle s = fullRender (mode s) (lineLength s) (ribbonsPerLine s)
436	                txtPrinter ""
437	{-# INLINE renderStyle #-}
438	
439	-- | Default TextDetails printer.
440	txtPrinter :: TextDetails -> String -> String
441	txtPrinter (Chr c)   s  = c:s
442	txtPrinter (Str s1)  s2 = s1 ++ s2
443	txtPrinter (PStr s1) s2 = s1 ++ s2
444	
445	-- | The general rendering interface. Please refer to the @Style@ and @Mode@
446	-- types for a description of rendering mode, line length and ribbons.
447	fullRender :: Mode                     -- ^ Rendering mode.
448	           -> Int                      -- ^ Line length.
449	           -> Float                    -- ^ Ribbons per line.
450	           -> (TextDetails -> a -> a)  -- ^ What to do with text.
451	           -> a                        -- ^ What to do at the end.
452	           -> Doc                      -- ^ The document.
453	           -> a                        -- ^ Result.
454	fullRender m lineLen ribbons txt rest (Doc doc)
455	  = Ann.fullRender m lineLen ribbons txt rest doc
456	{-# INLINE fullRender #-}
457	
458

85	import Control.DeepSeq ( NFData(rnf) )
86	import Data.Function   ( on )
87	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 803
88	import Prelude         hiding ( (<>) )
89	#endif
90	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 800
91	import qualified Data.Semigroup as Semi ( Semigroup((<>)) )
92	#elif __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ < 709
93	import Data.Monoid     ( Monoid(mempty, mappend)  )
94	#endif
95	import Data.String     ( IsString(fromString) )

97	import GHC.Generics
98	
99	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
100	-- The Doc calculus
101	
102	{-
103	Laws for $$
104	~~~~~~~~~~~
105	<a1>    (x $$ y) $$ z   = x $$ (y $$ z)
106	<a2>    empty $$ x      = x
107	<a3>    x $$ empty      = x
108	
109	        ...ditto $+$...
110	
111	Laws for <>
112	~~~~~~~~~~~
113	<b1>    (x <> y) <> z   = x <> (y <> z)
114	<b2>    empty <> x      = empty
115	<b3>    x <> empty      = x
116	
117	        ...ditto <+>...
118	
119	Laws for text
120	~~~~~~~~~~~~~
121	<t1>    text s <> text t        = text (s++t)
122	<t2>    text "" <> x            = x, if x non-empty
123	
124	** because of law n6, t2 only holds if x doesn't
125	** start with `nest'.
126	
127	
128	Laws for nest
129	~~~~~~~~~~~~~
130	<n1>    nest 0 x                = x
131	<n2>    nest k (nest k' x)      = nest (k+k') x
132	<n3>    nest k (x <> y)         = nest k x <> nest k y
133	<n4>    nest k (x $$ y)         = nest k x $$ nest k y
134	<n5>    nest k empty            = empty
135	<n6>    x <> nest k y           = x <> y, if x non-empty
136	
137	** Note the side condition on <n6>!  It is this that
138	** makes it OK for empty to be a left unit for <>.
139	
140	Miscellaneous
141	~~~~~~~~~~~~~
142	<m1>    (text s <> x) $$ y = text s <> ((text "" <> x) $$
143	                                         nest (-length s) y)
144	
145	<m2>    (x $$ y) <> z = x $$ (y <> z)
146	        if y non-empty
147	
148	
149	Laws for list versions
150	~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
151	<l1>    sep (ps++[empty]++qs)   = sep (ps ++ qs)
152	        ...ditto hsep, hcat, vcat, fill...
153	
154	<l2>    nest k (sep ps) = sep (map (nest k) ps)
155	        ...ditto hsep, hcat, vcat, fill...
156	
157	Laws for oneLiner
158	~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
159	<o1>    oneLiner (nest k p) = nest k (oneLiner p)
160	<o2>    oneLiner (x <> y)   = oneLiner x <> oneLiner y
161	
162	You might think that the following verion of <m1> would
163	be neater:
164	
165	<3 NO>  (text s <> x) $$ y = text s <> ((empty <> x)) $$
166	                                         nest (-length s) y)
167	
168	But it doesn't work, for if x=empty, we would have
169	
170	        text s $$ y = text s <> (empty $$ nest (-length s) y)
171	                    = text s <> nest (-length s) y
172	-}
173	
174	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
175	-- Operator fixity
176	
177	infixl 6 <>
178	infixl 6 <+>
179	infixl 5 $$, $+$
180	
181	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
182	-- The Doc data type
183	
184	-- | The abstract type of documents. A Doc represents a /set/ of layouts. A Doc
185	-- with no occurrences of Union or NoDoc represents just one layout.
186	data Doc a
187	  = Empty                                            -- ^ An empty span, see 'empty'.
188	  | NilAbove (Doc a)                                 -- ^ @text "" $$ x@.
189	  | TextBeside !(AnnotDetails a) (Doc a)             -- ^ @text s <> x@.
190	  | Nest {-# UNPACK #-} !Int (Doc a)                 -- ^ @nest k x@.
191	  | Union (Doc a) (Doc a)                            -- ^ @ul `union` ur@.
192	  | NoDoc                                            -- ^ The empty set of documents.
193	  | Beside (Doc a) Bool (Doc a)                      -- ^ True <=> space between.
194	  | Above (Doc a) Bool (Doc a)                       -- ^ True <=> never overlap.
195	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 701
196	  deriving (Generic)
197	#endif
198	
199	{-
200	Here are the invariants:
201	
202	1) The argument of NilAbove is never Empty. Therefore a NilAbove occupies at
203	least two lines.
204	
205	2) The argument of @TextBeside@ is never @Nest@.
206	
207	3) The layouts of the two arguments of @Union@ both flatten to the same string.
208	
209	4) The arguments of @Union@ are either @TextBeside@, or @NilAbove@.
210	
211	5) A @NoDoc@ may only appear on the first line of the left argument of an
212	   union. Therefore, the right argument of an union can never be equivalent to
213	   the empty set (@NoDoc@).
214	
215	6) An empty document is always represented by @Empty@. It can't be hidden
216	   inside a @Nest@, or a @Union@ of two @Empty@s.
217	
218	7) The first line of every layout in the left argument of @Union@ is longer
219	   than the first line of any layout in the right argument. (1) ensures that
220	   the left argument has a first line. In view of (3), this invariant means
221	   that the right argument must have at least two lines.
222	
223	Notice the difference between
224	   * NoDoc (no documents)
225	   * Empty (one empty document; no height and no width)
226	   * text "" (a document containing the empty string; one line high, but has no
227	              width)
228	-}
229	
230	
231	-- | RDoc is a "reduced GDoc", guaranteed not to have a top-level Above or Beside.
232	type RDoc = Doc
233	
234	-- | An annotation (side-metadata) attached at a particular point in a @Doc@.
235	-- Allows carrying non-pretty-printed data around in a @Doc@ that is attached
236	-- at particular points in the structure. Once the @Doc@ is render to an output
237	-- type (such as 'String'), we can also retrieve where in the rendered document
238	-- our annotations start and end (see 'Span' and 'renderSpans').
239	data AnnotDetails a = AnnotStart
240	                    | NoAnnot !TextDetails {-# UNPACK #-} !Int
241	                    | AnnotEnd a
242	                      deriving (Show,Eq)
243	
244	instance Functor AnnotDetails where
245	  fmap _ AnnotStart     = AnnotStart
246	  fmap _ (NoAnnot d i)  = NoAnnot d i
247	  fmap f (AnnotEnd a)   = AnnotEnd (f a)
248	
249	-- NOTE: Annotations are assumed to have zero length; only text has a length.
250	annotSize :: AnnotDetails a -> Int
251	annotSize (NoAnnot _ l) = l
252	annotSize _             = 0
253	
254	-- | A TextDetails represents a fragment of text that will be output at some
255	-- point in a @Doc@.
256	data TextDetails = Chr  {-# UNPACK #-} !Char -- ^ A single Char fragment
257	                 | Str  String -- ^ A whole String fragment
258	                 | PStr String -- ^ Used to represent a Fast String fragment
259	                               --   but now deprecated and identical to the
260	                               --   Str constructor.
261	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 701
262	                 deriving (Show, Eq, Generic)
263	#endif
264	
265	-- Combining @Doc@ values
266	#if __GLASGOW_HASKELL__ >= 800
267	instance Semi.Semigroup (Doc a) where
268	#ifndef TESTING
269	    (<>) = (Text.PrettyPrint.Annotated.HughesPJ.<>)
270	#else
271	    (<>) = (PrettyTestVersion.<>)
272	#endif
273	
274	instance Monoid (Doc a) where
275	    mempty  = empty
276	    mappend = (Semi.<>)
277	#else
278	instance Monoid (Doc a) where
279	    mempty  = empty
280	    mappend = (<>)
281	#endif
282	
283	instance IsString (Doc a) where
284	    fromString = text
285	
286	instance Show (Doc a) where
287	  showsPrec _ doc cont = fullRender (mode style) (lineLength style)
288	                                    (ribbonsPerLine style)
289	                                    txtPrinter cont doc
290	
291	instance Eq (Doc a) where
292	  (==) = (==) `on` render
293	
294	instance Functor Doc where
295	  fmap _ Empty               = Empty
296	  fmap f (NilAbove d)        = NilAbove (fmap f d)
297	  fmap f (TextBeside td d)   = TextBeside (fmap f td) (fmap f d)
298	  fmap f (Nest k d)          = Nest k (fmap f d)
299	  fmap f (Union ur ul)       = Union (fmap f ur) (fmap f ul)
300	  fmap _ NoDoc               = NoDoc
301	  fmap f (Beside ld s rd)    = Beside (fmap f ld) s (fmap f rd)
302	  fmap f (Above ud s ld)     = Above (fmap f ud) s (fmap f ld)
303	
304	instance NFData a => NFData (Doc a) where
305	  rnf Empty               = ()
306	  rnf (NilAbove d)        = rnf d
307	  rnf (TextBeside td d)   = rnf td `seq` rnf d
308	  rnf (Nest k d)          = rnf k  `seq` rnf d
309	  rnf (Union ur ul)       = rnf ur `seq` rnf ul
310	  rnf NoDoc               = ()
311	  rnf (Beside ld s rd)    = rnf ld `seq` rnf s `seq` rnf rd
312	  rnf (Above ud s ld)     = rnf ud `seq` rnf s `seq` rnf ld
313	
314	instance NFData a => NFData (AnnotDetails a) where
315	  rnf AnnotStart     = ()
316	  rnf (NoAnnot d sl) = rnf d `seq` rnf sl
317	  rnf (AnnotEnd a)   = rnf a
318	
319	instance NFData TextDetails where
320	  rnf (Chr c)    = rnf c
321	  rnf (Str str)  = rnf str
322	  rnf (PStr str) = rnf str
323	
324	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
325	-- Values and Predicates on GDocs and TextDetails
326	
327	-- | Attach an annotation to a document.
328	annotate :: a -> Doc a -> Doc a
329	annotate a d = TextBeside AnnotStart
330	             $ beside (reduceDoc d) False
331	             $ TextBeside (AnnotEnd a) Empty
332	
333	
334	-- | A document of height and width 1, containing a literal character.
335	char :: Char -> Doc a
336	char c = textBeside_ (NoAnnot (Chr c) 1) Empty
337	
338	-- | A document of height 1 containing a literal string.
339	-- 'text' satisfies the following laws:
340	--
341	-- * @'text' s '<>' 'text' t = 'text' (s'++'t)@
342	--
343	-- * @'text' \"\" '<>' x = x@, if @x@ non-empty
344	--
345	-- The side condition on the last law is necessary because @'text' \"\"@
346	-- has height 1, while 'empty' has no height.
347	text :: String -> Doc a
348	text s = case length s of {sl -> textBeside_ (NoAnnot (Str s) sl) Empty}
349	
350	-- | Same as @text@. Used to be used for Bytestrings.
351	ptext :: String -> Doc a
352	ptext s = case length s of {sl -> textBeside_ (NoAnnot (PStr s) sl) Empty}
353	
354	-- | Some text with any width. (@text s = sizedText (length s) s@)
355	sizedText :: Int -> String -> Doc a
356	sizedText l s = textBeside_ (NoAnnot (Str s) l) Empty
357	
358	-- | Some text, but without any width. Use for non-printing text
359	-- such as a HTML or Latex tags
360	zeroWidthText :: String -> Doc a
361	zeroWidthText = sizedText 0
362	
363	-- | The empty document, with no height and no width.
364	-- 'empty' is the identity for '<>', '<+>', '$$' and '$+$', and anywhere
365	-- in the argument list for 'sep', 'hcat', 'hsep', 'vcat', 'fcat' etc.
366	empty :: Doc a
367	empty = Empty
368	
369	-- | Returns 'True' if the document is empty
370	isEmpty :: Doc a -> Bool
371	isEmpty Empty = True
372	isEmpty _     = False
373	
374	-- | Produce spacing for indenting the amount specified.
375	--
376	-- an old version inserted tabs being 8 columns apart in the output.
377	indent :: Int -> String
378	indent !n = replicate n ' '
379	
380	{-
381	Q: What is the reason for negative indentation (i.e. argument to indent
382	   is < 0) ?
383	
384	A:
385	This indicates an error in the library client's code.
386	If we compose a <> b, and the first line of b is more indented than some
387	other lines of b, the law <n6> (<> eats nests) may cause the pretty
388	printer to produce an invalid layout:
389	
390	doc       |0123345
391	------------------
392	d1        |a...|
393	d2        |...b|
394	          |c...|
395	
396	d1<>d2    |ab..|
397	         c|....|
398	
399	Consider a <> b, let `s' be the length of the last line of `a', `k' the
400	indentation of the first line of b, and `k0' the indentation of the
401	left-most line b_i of b.
402	
403	The produced layout will have negative indentation if `k - k0 > s', as
404	the first line of b will be put on the (s+1)th column, effectively
405	translating b horizontally by (k-s). Now if the i^th line of b has an
406	indentation k0 < (k-s), it is translated out-of-page, causing
407	`negative indentation'.
408	-}
409	
410	
411	semi   :: Doc a -- ^ A ';' character
412	comma  :: Doc a -- ^ A ',' character
413	colon  :: Doc a -- ^ A ':' character
414	space  :: Doc a -- ^ A space character
415	equals :: Doc a -- ^ A '=' character
416	lparen :: Doc a -- ^ A '(' character
417	rparen :: Doc a -- ^ A ')' character
418	lbrack :: Doc a -- ^ A '[' character
419	rbrack :: Doc a -- ^ A ']' character
420	lbrace :: Doc a -- ^ A '{' character
421	rbrace :: Doc a -- ^ A '}' character
422	semi   = char ';'
423	comma  = char ','
424	colon  = char ':'
425	space  = char ' '
426	equals = char '='
427	lparen = char '('
428	rparen = char ')'
429	lbrack = char '['
430	rbrack = char ']'
431	lbrace = char '{'
432	rbrace = char '}'
433	
434	spaceText, nlText :: AnnotDetails a
435	spaceText = NoAnnot (Chr ' ') 1
436	nlText    = NoAnnot (Chr '\n') 1
437	
438	int      :: Int      -> Doc a -- ^ @int n = text (show n)@
439	integer  :: Integer  -> Doc a -- ^ @integer n = text (show n)@
440	float    :: Float    -> Doc a -- ^ @float n = text (show n)@
441	double   :: Double   -> Doc a -- ^ @double n = text (show n)@
442	rational :: Rational -> Doc a -- ^ @rational n = text (show n)@
443	int      n = text (show n)
444	integer  n = text (show n)
445	float    n = text (show n)
446	double   n = text (show n)
447	rational n = text (show n)
448	
449	parens       :: Doc a -> Doc a -- ^ Wrap document in @(...)@
450	brackets     :: Doc a -> Doc a -- ^ Wrap document in @[...]@
451	braces       :: Doc a -> Doc a -- ^ Wrap document in @{...}@
452	quotes       :: Doc a -> Doc a -- ^ Wrap document in @\'...\'@
453	doubleQuotes :: Doc a -> Doc a -- ^ Wrap document in @\"...\"@
454	quotes p       = char '\'' <> p <> char '\''
455	doubleQuotes p = char '"' <> p <> char '"'
456	parens p       = char '(' <> p <> char ')'
457	brackets p     = char '[' <> p <> char ']'
458	braces p       = char '{' <> p <> char '}'
459	
460	-- | Apply 'parens' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
461	maybeParens :: Bool -> Doc a -> Doc a
462	maybeParens False = id
463	maybeParens True = parens
464	
465	-- | Apply 'brackets' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
466	maybeBrackets :: Bool -> Doc a -> Doc a
467	maybeBrackets False = id
468	maybeBrackets True = brackets
469	
470	-- | Apply 'braces' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
471	maybeBraces :: Bool -> Doc a -> Doc a
472	maybeBraces False = id
473	maybeBraces True = braces
474	
475	-- | Apply 'quotes' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
476	maybeQuotes :: Bool -> Doc a -> Doc a
477	maybeQuotes False = id
478	maybeQuotes True = quotes
479	
480	-- | Apply 'doubleQuotes' to 'Doc' if boolean is true.
481	maybeDoubleQuotes :: Bool -> Doc a -> Doc a
482	maybeDoubleQuotes False = id
483	maybeDoubleQuotes True = doubleQuotes
484	
485	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
486	-- Structural operations on GDocs
487	
488	-- | Perform some simplification of a built up @GDoc@.
489	reduceDoc :: Doc a -> RDoc a
490	reduceDoc (Beside p g q) = beside p g (reduceDoc q)
491	reduceDoc (Above  p g q) = above  p g (reduceDoc q)
492	reduceDoc p              = p
493	
494	-- | List version of '<>'.
495	hcat :: [Doc a] -> Doc a
496	hcat = snd . reduceHoriz . foldr (\p q -> Beside p False q) empty
497	
498	-- | List version of '<+>'.
499	hsep :: [Doc a] -> Doc a
500	hsep = snd . reduceHoriz . foldr (\p q -> Beside p True q)  empty
501	
502	-- | List version of '$$'.
503	vcat :: [Doc a] -> Doc a
504	vcat = snd . reduceVert . foldr (\p q -> Above p False q) empty
505	
506	-- | Nest (or indent) a document by a given number of positions
507	-- (which may also be negative).  'nest' satisfies the laws:
508	--
509	-- * @'nest' 0 x = x@
510	--
511	-- * @'nest' k ('nest' k' x) = 'nest' (k+k') x@
512	--
513	-- * @'nest' k (x '<>' y) = 'nest' k z '<>' 'nest' k y@
514	--
515	-- * @'nest' k (x '$$' y) = 'nest' k x '$$' 'nest' k y@
516	--
517	-- * @'nest' k 'empty' = 'empty'@
518	--
519	-- * @x '<>' 'nest' k y = x '<>' y@, if @x@ non-empty
520	--
521	-- The side condition on the last law is needed because
522	-- 'empty' is a left identity for '<>'.
523	nest :: Int -> Doc a -> Doc a
524	nest k p = mkNest k (reduceDoc p)
525	
526	-- | @hang d1 n d2 = sep [d1, nest n d2]@
527	hang :: Doc a -> Int -> Doc a -> Doc a
528	hang d1 n d2 = sep [d1, nest n d2]
529	
530	-- | @punctuate p [d1, ... dn] = [d1 \<> p, d2 \<> p, ... dn-1 \<> p, dn]@
531	punctuate :: Doc a -> [Doc a] -> [Doc a]
532	punctuate _ []     = []
533	punctuate p (x:xs) = go x xs
534	                   where go y []     = [y]
535	                         go y (z:zs) = (y <> p) : go z zs
536	
537	-- mkNest checks for Nest's invariant that it doesn't have an Empty inside it
538	mkNest :: Int -> Doc a -> Doc a
539	mkNest k _ | k `seq` False = undefined
540	mkNest k (Nest k1 p)       = mkNest (k + k1) p
541	mkNest _ NoDoc             = NoDoc
542	mkNest _ Empty             = Empty
543	mkNest 0 p                 = p
544	mkNest k p                 = nest_ k p
545	
546	-- mkUnion checks for an empty document
547	mkUnion :: Doc a -> Doc a -> Doc a
548	mkUnion Empty _ = Empty
549	mkUnion p q     = p `union_` q
550	
551	data IsEmpty = IsEmpty | NotEmpty
552	
553	reduceHoriz :: Doc a -> (IsEmpty, Doc a)
554	reduceHoriz (Beside p g q) = eliminateEmpty Beside (snd (reduceHoriz p)) g (reduceHoriz q)
555	reduceHoriz doc            = (NotEmpty, doc)
556	
557	reduceVert :: Doc a -> (IsEmpty, Doc a)
558	reduceVert (Above  p g q) = eliminateEmpty Above  (snd (reduceVert p)) g (reduceVert q)
559	reduceVert doc            = (NotEmpty, doc)
560	
561	{-# INLINE eliminateEmpty #-}
562	eliminateEmpty ::
563	  (Doc a -> Bool -> Doc a -> Doc a) ->
564	  Doc a -> Bool -> (IsEmpty, Doc a) -> (IsEmpty, Doc a)
565	eliminateEmpty _    Empty _ q          = q
566	eliminateEmpty cons p     g q          =
567	  (NotEmpty,
568	   -- We're not empty whether or not q is empty, so for laziness-sake,
569	   -- after checking that p isn't empty, we put the NotEmpty result
570	   -- outside independent of q. This allows reduceAB to immediately
571	   -- return the appropriate constructor (Above or Beside) without
572	   -- forcing the entire nested Doc. This allows the foldr in vcat,
573	   -- hsep, and hcat to be lazy on its second argument, avoiding a
574	   -- stack overflow.
575	   case q of
576	     (NotEmpty, q') -> cons p g q'
577	     (IsEmpty, _) -> p)
578	
579	nilAbove_ :: RDoc a -> RDoc a
580	nilAbove_ = NilAbove
581	
582	-- | Arg of a TextBeside is always an RDoc.
583	textBeside_ :: AnnotDetails a -> RDoc a -> RDoc a
584	textBeside_  = TextBeside
585	
586	nest_ :: Int -> RDoc a -> RDoc a
587	nest_ = Nest
588	
589	union_ :: RDoc a -> RDoc a -> RDoc a
590	union_ = Union
